2. Second MOUSE on right green (row 31, col 28): dots swapped (left W->c, right c->W)
3. Third MOUSE on left green (row 34, col 22): dots changed (left c->N, right W->c)
4. Fourth MOUSE on left green (row 33, col 22): no change
Wait, maybe the fourth MOUSE didn't work because the green box shape changed. The left green was at (33-34, 21-23), which is a 2x3 box. The center would be at (33.5, 22), so (33, 22) is inside the box. But maybe the MOUSE needs to hit the center of the box, not just any cell.
Actually, let me look at the animation metadata to see if the MOUSE action had any effect.

Let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to figure out the exact mechanics, let me look at the history to find the initial state and see what a minimal solution might look like.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# MOUSE at (33,22) had NO effect - dots unchanged
# Let me check the animation and also look at early history to understand the game

# Check if there was any animation
print(f"Animation info: {last_action_result.get('animation', {})}")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
print(f"Gameplay changed: {last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
print(f"No impact: {last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")

# Look at initial state
if len(history) > 0:
    init_seg = history[0].frame.segmentation['nodes']
    print(f"\nInitial state (history[0]):")
    for n in init_seg:
        if n['color'] == 'N':
            print(f"  Green: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
        if n['color'] == 'c' and 10 <= n['pixels'] <= 20:
            print(f"  Charcoal: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check all transitions to find any level completion
print(f"\nTotal transitions: {len(transitions)}")
for i, t in enumerate(transitions):
    if t.result.get('level_completed') or t.result.get('done'):
        print(f"  Transition {i}: {t.action} -> level_completed={t.result.get('level_completed')}, done={t.result.get('done')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

- Right green: (36-38, 21-23) with c - 3x3 = 9 pixels (but segmentation says 8?)

But the initial state has both green boxes with 8 pixels, which means they're both 3x3 with 1-pixel dots.

Let me just check the current segmentation to see the pixel counts.

But the left green is not the leftmost (the right green is at col 21-23, same column). So I need to figure out which green box UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT moves.
Actually, both green boxes are at col 21-23. The left green is at row 33-34, and the right green is at row 36-38. So they're at the same column but different rows. The rule is "leftmost", and they're tied. The tie-breaker might be "topmost", so the left green (row 33-34) is the topmost, and UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT moves the left green.

# Both greens at col 21-23, left at row 33-34, right at row 36-38
# If tie-break is topmost, LEFT should move left green

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, hash={n['hash']}")
    if n['color'] == 'c' and 10 <= n['pixels'] <= 20:
        print(f"Charcoal: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Try LEFT to see which green moves

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("\nAfter LEFT:")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
    if n['color'] == 'c' and 10 <= n['pixels'] <= 20:

</parameter>
</function>
